result:
The old woman at the bus stop was feeding pigeons again, tossing crumbs from a bag that seemed to never empty. I'd been watching her for three weeks now, every morning at 7:42, and the bag never got smaller. The pigeons knew her schedule better than the city buses did – they'd start gathering at 7:30, cooing and strutting in anticipation.

I wasn't supposed to notice things like that. My therapist said I needed to stop looking for patterns everywhere, stop assuming everything meant something. "Sometimes a coincidence is just a coincidence," she'd say, clicking her pen in a rhythm that I counted: click-click-pause-click. Every session. Same pattern.

But the woman with the pigeons wasn't a coincidence. Neither was the fact that every pigeon had a tiny splash of white on its left wing. I'd checked. Got close enough to see, which meant getting close enough for the woman to notice me noticing.

"They're not really pigeons," she said one Tuesday, not looking up from her crumb-tossing.

I should have walked away. Should have caught my bus and gone to work and forgotten about impossible bags and marked birds. Instead, I sat down beside her.

"What are they then?"

"Messages," she said. "Lost ones. Every text someone typed but didn't send, every letter someone wrote but burned, every word someone almost said but swallowed instead. They have to go somewhere."

She handed me the bag. It weighed nothing and everything at once, like holding a handful of regrets. Inside were what looked like breadcrumbs but felt like whispers against my palm.

"Try it," she said.

I took a pinch and tossed it. A pigeon with that telltale white mark pecked at it, and suddenly I heard my father's voice, clear as morning: "I'm proud of you." Words he'd never said. Words I'd found written on a napkin in his jacket pocket after the funeral, never sent, never spoken, just carried around until paper met washing machine and the ink bled away.

The pigeon looked at me with eyes too knowing for a bird, then flew away, carrying something I couldn't name but felt leaving my chest.

"Heavy, aren't they?" the woman said. "The unsaid things. They accumulate. Build up like sediment in the soul. That's why I feed them. Give them form, give them flight, let them go."

"How long have you been doing this?"

She smiled, and her teeth were too white, too many. "Since the first person thought better of speaking. Since the first secret was kept. Since silence became heavier than sound."

I wanted to ask if she was crazy or if I was, but those seemed like the wrong questions. Instead, I asked, "Can I help?"

She stood, pressing the bag into my hands. "I've been waiting for someone to ask." 

Then she walked away, and I noticed she didn't leave footprints in the frost.

The pigeons stayed, watching me with their impossible eyes. I reached into the bag and felt the weight of every message never sent. My ex-girlfriend's apology. My mother's confession. My own desperate pleading to a universe that never answered back – until now.

I started tossing crumbs, and the pigeons descended like thoughts, like chances, like grace. Each one that ate and flew away took something with it, something I hadn't realized I'd been carrying. The bag never emptied, but I began to feel lighter.

A man in a suit stopped, watched me feeding the birds. "Crazy old lady used to do this," he said. "Every morning."

"Yeah," I said, not correcting his tense. "Someone should."

He checked his watch, almost walked away, then turned back. "My daughter... I never told her..." He stopped, shook his head. "Forget it."

I held out the bag. "Want to feed them? Just once?"

He looked at me like I was insane, but something in his eyes recognized something in mine. He took a handful of crumbs, threw them high into the air. The pigeons swirled up like smoke, like signals, like prayers with wings. When they settled, he was crying.

"I heard her," he said. "She said she forgave me."

"The pigeons?"

"No," he said, wiping his eyes. "Well, yes. But also..." He couldn't finish, but he didn't need to. I understood. The unsaid things go both ways – what we never told them, what they never told us, an endless loop of silence that these strange birds somehow broke.

He left lighter too. I could see it in his walk.

I've been feeding them for six months now. Every morning, 7:42. The bag never empties, never gets heavier, just stays exactly as needed. People stop sometimes, drawn by something they can't name. Some take a handful of crumbs. Some just watch. Some cry.

Yesterday, a child asked me if the pigeons were magic.

"No," I said, then reconsidered. "Or maybe magic is just the word for things that carry what we can't."

She seemed satisfied with that, helped me scatter crumbs until her mother called her away. 

The woman never came back, but sometimes I see her reflection in store windows, nodding approval. Sometimes the pigeons form patterns in the sky that look like words in languages I don't speak but somehow understand.

My therapist says I seem lighter lately. I don't tell her about the pigeons. Some things are better left unsaid – until they need to be fed to birds that aren't really birds, in a morning ritual that's older than memory and newer than each dawn.

Tomorrow I'll be there again, 7:42, with a bag that weighs nothing and everything. The pigeons will be waiting. So will the unsaid things.

So will I.
